expanded_description: Alkanes represent the simplest family of hydrocarbons, containing only single bonds between carbon atoms. This section explores their molecular structure, three-dimensional geometry, systematic IUPAC nomenclature, and physical properties that arise from their saturated nature.

core_concept.principle: Alkanes Are Saturated Hydrocarbons with Maximum Hydrogen Content
core_concept.explanation: Alkanes contain exclusively carbon-carbon single bonds ($\text{C}-\text{C}$) and carbon-hydrogen bonds ($\text{C}-\text{H}$), making them 'saturated' with hydrogen atoms. Each carbon atom is $sp^3$ hybridized with tetrahedral geometry, resulting in the general molecular formula $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2}$ for acyclic alkanes. This saturation makes alkanes relatively unreactive compared to unsaturated hydrocarbons.

image.alt: Diagram showing alkane molecular structure with tetrahedral carbon centers
image.caption: Alkanes feature $sp^3$ hybridized carbons in tetrahedral geometry, with all bonds being $\sigma$-bonds allowing free rotation.

sections.introduction: Alkanes are the foundational family of organic compounds, consisting entirely of carbon and hydrogen atoms connected exclusively by single covalent bonds. They are called 'saturated' hydrocarbons because each carbon atom is bonded to the maximum possible number of hydrogen atoms. Alkanes serve as the reference point for understanding all other organic compound families, and their systematic naming forms the basis of IUPAC nomenclature for more complex molecules.

key_concept.definition: An alkane is a saturated acyclic hydrocarbon with the general molecular formula $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2}$, where $n$ represents the number of carbon atoms. All carbon-carbon bonds are single $\sigma$-bonds, and each carbon is $sp^3$ hybridized with tetrahedral geometry.
key_concept.context: Alkanes are also known as 'paraffins' (from Latin 'parum affinis' meaning 'little affinity') due to their low chemical reactivity. They form a homologous series where each successive member differs by a $\text{CH}_2$ unit.

image.alt: Homologous series of alkanes from methane to pentane
image.caption: The alkane homologous series: methane ($\text{CH}_4$), ethane ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$), propane ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$), butane ($\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$), pentane ($\text{C}_5\text{H}_{12}$).

principles.statement: For every $n$ carbon atoms in an acyclic alkane, there are exactly $2n+2$ hydrogen atoms, giving the general formula $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2}$.
principles.application: This formula allows quick determination of molecular formula from carbon count, or verification of whether a hydrocarbon is a saturated alkane. Any deviation indicates unsaturation (double/triple bonds) or cyclic structure.
principles.analogy: Think of the alkane formula as a 'fully loaded' carbon chain: each interior carbon carries 2 hydrogens, while the two end carbons each carry 3 hydrogens, giving $2n$ (interior) + 2 (ends) = $2n+2$ total hydrogens.
principles.example: For decane ($n=10$): $\text{H count} = 2(10)+2 = 22$, so molecular formula is $\text{C}_{10}\text{H}_{22}$.

principles.statement: Each $sp^3$ hybridized carbon in an alkane adopts tetrahedral geometry with bond angles of approximately $109.5^\circ$.
principles.application: This geometry minimizes electron-pair repulsion (VSEPR theory) and determines the three-dimensional shape of alkane molecules. It also explains why carbon chains are not linear but zigzag in their extended conformation.
principles.analogy: Imagine carbon as the center of a pyramid with four triangular faces: bonds extend to each corner, creating maximum separation between bonding electron pairs.
principles.example: In propane ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$), the $\text{C}-\text{C}-\text{C}$ bond angle is $\approx 109.5^\circ$, creating a bent chain rather than a straight line.

components.definition: Alkanes consist of a carbon skeleton (chain of $sp^3$ carbons) with hydrogen atoms saturating all remaining valences. The $\text{C}-\text{C}$ bond length is approximately $154 \text{ pm}$ and $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bond length is approximately $109 \text{ pm}$.
components.interpretation: The $+2$ in the formula accounts for the two terminal carbons each having one extra hydrogen compared to interior carbons. For $n=1$ (methane), all 4 bonds are $\text{C}-\text{H}$; for $n \geq 2$, there are $(n-1)$ $\text{C}-\text{C}$ bonds and $(2n+2)$ $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bonds.

image.alt: Diagram showing alkane bond lengths and angles
image.caption: Alkane structural parameters: $\text{C}-\text{C} = 154 \text{ pm}$, $\text{C}-\text{H} = 109 \text{ pm}$, bond angle $= 109.5^\circ$.

sections.introduction: The International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) developed a systematic naming system that allows any alkane structure to be named unambiguously, and any alkane name to be converted to its unique structure. For straight-chain (unbranched) alkanes, naming follows a simple pattern based on the number of carbon atoms in the continuous chain.

classifications.value: Based on number of carbon atoms in the longest continuous chain
classifications.behavior: The prefix indicates chain length; combined with suffix '-ane' for alkanes, it creates the parent name. Memorizing prefixes 1-10 is essential for organic nomenclature.

image.caption: Methane: simplest alkane with one carbon atom.
Decane: 10-carbon straight-chain alkane, component of diesel fuel.

classifications.value: Indicates the homologous series (family) of the compound
classifications.behavior: The suffix immediately communicates the degree of saturation. For alkanes, '-ane' confirms all bonds are single bonds.

examples.explanation: Butane ($\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$) has '-ane' suffix indicating saturation; butene ($\text{C}_4\text{H}_8$) has '-ene' suffix indicating one $\text{C}=\text{C}$ double bond.

sections.key_concept: IUPAC alkane names = Prefix (carbon count) + Suffix ('-ane'). Memorize: meth-, eth-, prop-, but-, pent-, hex-, hept-, oct-, non-, dec- for 1-10 carbons.

sections.introduction: Alkanes can be represented in multiple ways, each serving different purposes. Understanding these representations is crucial for visualizing molecular structure, communicating with other chemists, and solving nomenclature problems.

categories.description: Shows only the number and type of atoms (e.g., $\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$ for butane).
categories.detailed_mechanism: Molecular formulas provide compositional information but no structural detail. Multiple isomers can share the same molecular formula, so this representation alone cannot distinguish between structural variants.

examples.explanation: Molecular formula: $\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$ — tells us 4 carbons and 10 hydrogens, but not how they connect.

categories.description: Shows atom connectivity in a linear text format (e.g., $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$ for butane).
categories.detailed_mechanism: Condensed formulas show the sequence of carbon atoms and attached hydrogens without drawing bonds explicitly. Groups like $\text{CH}_3$ (methyl), $\text{CH}_2$ (methylene), and $\text{CH}$ (methine) are written consecutively.

examples.explanation: Condensed: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$ or $\text{CH}_3(\text{CH}_2)_3\text{CH}_3$ — shows 5-carbon chain with terminal methyl groups.

image.caption: Pentane condensed formula showing carbon sequence.

categories.description: Shows all atoms and all bonds explicitly as lines or electron pairs.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Lewis structures display every covalent bond and lone pair, providing complete bonding information. They are pedagogically useful but become unwieldy for larger molecules.

examples.explanation: Each $\text{C}-\text{C}$ and $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bond drawn as a line; all 8 hydrogen atoms shown explicitly around 3 carbons.

image.caption: Propane Lewis structure showing all bonds explicitly.

categories.description: Simplified representation where carbon atoms are vertices and hydrogens on carbons are implied.
categories.detailed_mechanism: In skeletal structures: (1) each vertex and line endpoint represents a carbon atom, (2) hydrogens attached to carbons are not shown (assumed to complete 4 bonds), (3) heteroatoms (O, N, Cl, etc.) are shown explicitly with their hydrogens. This is the most efficient representation for complex molecules.

examples.explanation: Drawn as a zigzag line with 5 segments (6 vertices = 6 carbons). Each vertex has implied hydrogens to satisfy tetravalency.

image.caption: Hexane skeletal structure: zigzag line with 6 carbon vertices.

sections.introduction: Although alkanes contain only single bonds, they are not static structures. Free rotation around $\text{C}-\text{C}$ $\sigma$-bonds allows alkanes to adopt different spatial arrangements called conformations. These conformations interconvert rapidly at room temperature but have different energies due to steric and torsional effects.

principles.statement: Single $\sigma$-bonds allow free rotation of attached groups, enabling multiple conformational arrangements without breaking bonds.
principles.application: This rotation creates conformational isomers (conformers) that differ only in the dihedral angle between substituents. Conformers are not isolable at room temperature due to rapid interconversion.
principles.analogy: Think of a $\text{C}-\text{C}$ bond as a rotating axle: groups attached to each carbon can spin around the axis, changing their relative positions like hands on a clock.
principles.example: In ethane ($\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}_3$), the two methyl groups can rotate relative to each other through $360^\circ$, creating infinite conformations.

principles.statement: When bonds on adjacent carbons align (eclipse), electron clouds repel, increasing energy. Staggered conformations minimize this repulsion.
principles.application: Torsional strain affects conformational stability: staggered conformations are lower energy (more stable) than eclipsed conformations. Energy difference in ethane is $\approx 12 \text{ kJ/mol}$.
principles.analogy: Eclipsed bonds are like crowded elevator passengers facing each other uncomfortably; staggered bonds are like passengers spaced evenly around the elevator wall.
principles.example: Ethane eclipsed conformation is $12 \text{ kJ/mol}$ higher in energy than staggered conformation due to $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bond eclipsing.

worked_examples.title: Newman Projection Analysis of Ethane
worked_examples.problem: Draw and compare the staggered and eclipsed conformations of ethane using Newman projections. Identify which is more stable and explain why.

step_by_step.explanation: Understand Newman projection: view the molecule along the $\text{C}-\text{C}$ bond axis. Front carbon is a point with three bonds radiating out; back carbon is a circle with three bonds radiating from the circumference.

Draw staggered conformation: front carbon $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bonds are positioned $60^\circ$ between back carbon $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bonds. Dihedral angle between any two $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bonds = $60^\circ$.
Draw eclipsed conformation: front carbon $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bonds align directly with back carbon $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bonds. Dihedral angle = $0^\circ$.
Compare energies: staggered has no torsional strain; eclipsed has three $\text{H}-\text{H}$ eclipsing interactions worth $\approx 4 \text{ kJ/mol}$ each.

worked_examples.final_answer: Staggered conformation is more stable by $12 \text{ kJ/mol}$. At room temperature, ethane molecules rapidly interconvert but spend more time in staggered conformations.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Newman projections visualize conformational energy differences arising from torsional strain in $\sigma$-bond rotation.

sections.key_concept: Conformations are different spatial arrangements from bond rotation (not isomers). Staggered = stable (low energy); Eclipsed = unstable (high energy). Energy barrier for ethane rotation = $12 \text{ kJ/mol}$.

sections.introduction: Alkane physical properties follow predictable trends based on molecular size and structure. Understanding these trends helps predict boiling points, melting points, solubility, and density for any alkane in the homologous series.

categories.description: Boiling points increase with molecular weight due to stronger London dispersion forces.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Alkanes are nonpolar, so intermolecular forces are exclusively London dispersion forces (induced dipole-induced dipole). Larger molecules have more electrons and greater surface area, increasing polarizability and dispersion force strength. Each $\text{CH}_2$ addition raises boiling point by $\approx 20-30^\circ\text{C}$.

examples.explanation: Methane ($-161.5^\circ\text{C}$) < Ethane ($-88.6^\circ\text{C}$) < Propane ($-42.1^\circ\text{C}$) < Butane ($-0.5^\circ\text{C}$) < Pentane ($36.1^\circ\text{C}$). C1-C4 are gases; C5-C17 are liquids; C18+ are waxy solids.

image.alt: Graph showing boiling point increase with carbon number
image.caption: Boiling point increases linearly with carbon chain length in alkanes.

categories.description: Branched alkanes have lower boiling points than straight-chain isomers.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Branching reduces molecular surface area, decreasing the area available for intermolecular contact. Reduced contact means weaker dispersion forces and lower boiling point. Branching also prevents efficient packing in solid state.

examples.explanation: n-Pentane ($36.1^\circ\text{C}$) > Isopentane ($27.8^\circ\text{C}$) > Neopentane ($9.5^\circ\text{C}$). Neopentane's spherical shape minimizes surface contact.

image.caption: Increased branching lowers boiling point: n-pentane > isopentane > neopentane.

categories.description: Alkanes are hydrophobic and less dense than water.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Alkanes are nonpolar and cannot form hydrogen bonds with water, making them immiscible ('like dissolves like'). Alkane densities range from $0.6-0.8 \text{ g/mL}$, less than water's $1.0 \text{ g/mL}$, so alkanes float on water.

examples.explanation: Hexane ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{14}$) forms a separate layer on water. Density = $0.66 \text{ g/mL}$. Used in liquid-liquid extractions to separate nonpolar compounds from aqueous solutions.

sections.section_title: Nomenclature Practice: From Name to Structure
sections.introduction: Mastering alkane nomenclature requires bidirectional fluency: converting names to structures and structures to names. This section provides systematic practice for interpreting IUPAC names and drawing corresponding structures.

worked_examples.title: Drawing 3-Ethyl-2-Methylhexane
worked_examples.problem: Draw the structural formula for 3-ethyl-2-methylhexane from its IUPAC name.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the parent chain: 'hexane' indicates 6 carbon atoms in the longest continuous chain.
Number the parent chain from the end that gives substituents the lowest possible numbers. Number left to right: positions 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6.
Add substituents at specified positions: '2-methyl' = $\text{CH}_3$ at carbon 2; '3-ethyl' = $\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$ at carbon 3.
Add hydrogens to satisfy tetravalency: each carbon must have 4 bonds total.

worked_examples.concept_applied: IUPAC naming rules: parent chain identification, numbering for lowest substituent positions, alphabetical ordering of substituents (ethyl before methyl).

image.caption: 3-Ethyl-2-methylhexane: 6-carbon parent with methyl at C2 and ethyl at C3.

worked_examples.title: Naming a Given Alkane Structure

step_by_step.explanation: Find the longest continuous carbon chain. Count all possible paths: horizontal chain = 6 carbons.
Number from the end giving lowest substituent numbers. Left-to-right: substituents at 3,5. Right-to-left: substituents at 2,4. Choose 2,4 (lower numbers).
Identify and name substituents: two methyl groups at positions 2 and 4.
Combine: substituent names + parent name. Use 'di-' prefix for two identical groups.

worked_examples.final_answer: IUPAC name: 2,4-dimethylhexane. Note: numbers separated by commas; numbers and letters separated by hyphens; no spaces in the name.
worked_examples.concept_applied: IUPAC rules: lowest locant set takes priority; identical substituents use multiplicative prefixes (di-, tri-, tetra-).

image.caption: 2,4-Dimethylhexane: hexane parent with methyl groups at positions 2 and 4.

sections.section_title: Homologous Series and Alkane Properties Table
sections.introduction: The first ten straight-chain alkanes form a homologous series with systematically varying properties. Memorizing these foundational compounds accelerates nomenclature proficiency and property prediction.

components.function: Natural gas primary component; simplest organic molecule
components.key_feature: Tetrahedral symmetry; gas at room temperature; $\text{BP} = -161.5^\circ\text{C}$

components.function: Natural gas component; ethylene production feedstock
LPG (liquefied petroleum gas); heating and cooking fuel
Lighter fuel; aerosol propellant; gasoline blending
Solvent; gasoline component; blowing agent for foams
components.key_feature: Three isomers; first liquid alkane at room temperature; $\text{BP} = 36.1^\circ\text{C}$

components.function: Industrial solvent; extraction of vegetable oils
components.key_feature: Five isomers; neurotoxic in high concentrations; $\text{BP} = 68.7^\circ\text{C}$

components.function: Gasoline component; octane rating reference (0 octane)
components.key_feature: Nine isomers; defines low end of fuel quality scale; $\text{BP} = 98.4^\circ\text{C}$

components.function: Gasoline component; octane rating reference (100 octane for isooctane)

summary: Alkanes are saturated hydrocarbons with general formula $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2}$, featuring exclusively $\text{C}-\text{C}$ and $\text{C}-\text{H}$ single bonds. Each carbon is $sp^3$ hybridized with tetrahedral geometry ($109.5^\circ$ bond angles). IUPAC nomenclature uses prefixes (meth-, eth-, prop-, but-, pent-, hex-, hept-, oct-, non-, dec-) combined with suffix '-ane' to name straight-chain alkanes. Multiple structural representations exist: molecular formula, condensed formula, Lewis structure, and skeletal (line-angle) structure. Free rotation around $\sigma$-bonds creates conformational isomers: staggered (stable, low energy) and eclipsed (unstable, high energy due to torsional strain). Physical properties follow trends: boiling point increases with chain length (stronger London dispersion forces) and decreases with branching (reduced surface area). The first ten alkanes (methane through decane) form a homologous series with systematically varying properties, serving as the foundation for all organic nomenclature.
